pullRequest.title: Fix history api race causing stale data
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3055

The bug/race is that:

- `sabnzbd.LAST_HISTORY_UPDATE` is read for the first check
- Builds the history response
- Reads `sabnzbd.LAST_HISTORY_UPDATE` again for the response

Between the two `LAST_HISTORY_UPDATE` reads it can increment and the client gets stale history data paired with the latest `LAST_HISTORY_UPDATE`, then subsequent calls it never gets the latest history.

Fix is to capture the counter once at the start.

Secondary issue is `history_updated` being called from multiple threads, without a lock it is not atomic so the concurrent calls can lose increments.

pullRequest.title: prevent cherrypy from stumbling over invalid cookies
pullRequest.body: closes #3078

pullRequest.title: Skip adding postproc items to the history if they are already completed
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3113

Is this what you were thinking?

Created the set to avoid looping through the list multiple times if several items are postprocessing.

It could be a little smarter by not creating the set if items is empty, or that could be in `build_history` and skip the `reverse + add_active_history + reverse`.

```py
    # Add the postproc items to the top of the history
    # Reverse the queue to add items to the top (faster than insert)
    if any(postproc_queue):
        items.reverse()
        add_active_history(postproc_queue, items)
        items.reverse()

    total_items += postproc_queue_size
```

I can add a test, I think I'd do something like `test_add_active_history_consistency` but try calling `add_active_history` multiple times with the same nzo then check the length.

pullRequest.title: Fix snap with core24
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3125 

I've only been able to test building this from amd64 and arm64.

Improvements include:

- Shrank snap from ~260MB to 20-30MB depending on platform, mostly from removing the whole git history in .git and a few other directories and binaries not needed at runtime
- Added `__baseline__` git commit hash if a clean git state, is not working in the existing snap
- Command tweaked to allow extra arguments [[SNAP, LMDE 6] sabnzbd don't allow commandline parameters](https://forums.sabnzbd.org/viewtopic.php?t=27049)
- Replaced par2cmdline with par2cmdline-turbo using latest release available, built from source and stripped
- Changed most yaml list formats, just a little easier to read and diff changes

~~I have only included 7za, because that's the only one I could see SAB finding so I could shrink the snap by removing the others and shared library, is that OK? or I can add 7z and 7z.so~~ switched to 7zip-standalone which has a single 7zz binary with better format support.

I've no idea what libdb5.3 (Berkeley DB) is needed for, a recent commit has changed it to libdb5.3-dev, I've left it as it was, but it also seems to run without it?

I couldn't get cross-compilation to work, so it'll need to be built from each architecture.

If cross-compilation is ever revisited:
- The python step would always install the requirements for the host architecture
- par2cmdline-turbo wouldn't be a problem just a few configure and environment variables to set
- snapcraft-preload not sure multilib won't be available which it only uses for the -m32 on x86_64 architecture anyway, so it should be OK but would need CMakeLists.txt changes to not use the host architecture.

So, it'll have to stay with snapcraft.io building per architecture or drop armhf and build it from GitHub runners.

pullRequest.title: Add option to bind outgoing connections
pullRequest.body: Closes https://github.com/sabnzbd/sabnzbd/issues/3153

pullRequest.title: Pipelining and performance optimisations
pullRequest.body:
I've had pipelining in the back of my mind for a couple of years; I just hadn't found the motivation to properly investigate and find a way to implement it.

There is an excellent explanation of the theory on https://github.com/sabnzbd/sabnzbd/issues/3171#issuecomment-3538705659 the general idea is that the current request => response loop is bottlenecked by latency, a lot of the time no data is being transmitted. 

[RFC 3977](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc3977) describes NNTP server support for pipelining. The general idea is that by having a constant stream of requests going out we better saturate the incoming bandwidth.

There are a few aspects to the pull request:
- Pipelining, which is intended to improve speeds for >1 Gbps connections or at high latency, use fewer connections, and therefore fewer resources
- Streaming/incremental decoding, rather than reading a whole response into a buffer before decoding it all at once, process small parts as they are received, this can use less memory, process data quicker because the decoded data to be crc'd is still in cpu caches
- Performance optimisations, primarily use of more appropriate data structures for their use-case, [TimeComplexity](https://wiki.python.org/moin/TimeComplexity) - motivation is to challenge that SAB is "slow" because it's in Python, I don't think it is there are just some non optimal choices from 10+ years ago before fast connections were more common place
- Thread safety, fixed a few but identified issues

Closes #3171 🚀 

## What has changed and why

This is kind of a lot, but I expected you might want to know why I've made many of these changes, especially as it touches 15+ year old code in places.

<details>
  <summary>Implementation specifics</summary>

- Config/Interface: added pipelining_requests to specials (may need a better name), default 5, in the future (maybe not this PR) to be replaced with an ever-changing calculation based on average request -> complete time
- Server:
    - **article_queue:** a sequence of prefetched articles assigned to a server
        - Changed to a deque, efficient O(1) pop and append operations; with lists pop(0) is O(n) it memmoves all the other elements [ref](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/b87613f21474ea848fec435cbfe63d8cb1c7c44c/Objects/listobject.c#L1558-L1561) so a prefetch of 20 items makes 20 memmoves for 190 elements in total; a deque is a doubly-linked list, appends and pops to either end are just setting a couple of pointers, in such a hot path this matters
        - Dequeue is thread safe (at least for append, pop, and len) - but locks may be required on occasion such as checking length before pop/peek
        - A single object instance is now used for the lifetime of the server, less constant creation, growing, and destruction of lists
    - **get_article(..., peek:bool = False):** I needed a way to peek at the next item without consuming it, only used to kickstart idle servers, if the socket isn't in a state to accept article requests yet it can't be added to command queue because it could interrupt auth.
    - **selectors:** replaces socket.select
        - Uses efficient select implementation for the platform
        - Lets us subscribe to availability for read and write events, without maintaining separate lists and combining results as with socket.select
- Decoder - one of two may need renaming:
    - Decoding of yEnc and UU are now handled by `sabctools.Decoder`
    - Decoder just copies required values out of the decoder metadata
    - May need to look at what happens for unknown formats, if that can happen they won't get to the decoder anyway.
- Downloader:
    - **__reset_nw => reset_nw:** yeah... I don't like this, it's mostly operations on a nw so I feel it should be a method on a nw but it needs the DOWNLOADER_LOCK, I needed access from NewsWrappper since it now writes requests.
    - **process_nw_queue.put_multiple:** no longer used, I profiled and it's now faster this way because we need `(NewsWrapper, event)`, use of MultiAddQueue could be replaced with queue.Queue but I wanted to keep the changes lean
    - **process_nw:** now reads from the socket in `process_nw_read` like before but in a loop to consume `sock.pending()`, second read only takes `sock.pending()` to avoid potentially reading from a single socket for too long. Much of the logic for dealing with the responses has moved to the NewsWrapper
    - **__finish_connect_nw => finish_connect_nw:** similar to the other one, it's now only used in NewsWrapper so could/should move but didn't want to complicate the PR
    - **__request_article:** removed, NewsWrapper needs to coordinate reads and writes
- NewsWrapper: The real meat of things
    - **command_queue and concurrent_requests:** the general idea is there is a queue of commands we want the server to send, and in-flight limits use a threading.Semaphore that is released once we have a whole response.
    - **send_buffer:** remainder of partially sent data, rare but allows us to make non-blocking writes and avoids potential deadlock when we can't write more until we read; sendall is basically a blocking while loop that doesn't terminate until it has all sent
    - **queue(command, article):** queues a command, blocks until space in the command queue
    - **command_complete:** not hugely in favour of the name, maybe completed_command?
        - Once a decoder has read a whole response, figure out what to do with it.
    - **recv_chunk => read(..., nbytes: int = 0):**
        - Per NewsWrapper buffer doesn't need to resize anymore, the decoder stores its streamed data internally, still enforce the limit against how many bytes a decoder has consumed to check something strange has not happened
        - nbytes is used when we want to limit to reading just sock.pending() and always uses the python default recv_into because we need to read <16 KiB anyway.
        - Tells sabctools how much data it needs to process, which handles all decoding and multiple responses per read
        - Reads back any number of completed responses from the decoder
    - **write:**
        - Writes command_queue and send_buffer to the socket, enqueues more requests to command_queue (I might make it limited loop, need to check if necessary - I doubt it), operates in a non-blocking manner that is if writes would block it stops so we can move on to the next socket
        - Handles idle connection detection - can write but nothing to write
    - **soft_reset:** removed, the NewsWrapper need to manage its state
- NzbQueue:
    - **get_articles:** including the chain from NzbQueue > NzbObject > NzbFile
        - Populates the server deque, instead of returning a new list instance
    - **register_article:** locks the nzf instead of copying the articles (I'll get onto this later)
- TryList
    - Adds a per object lock and can use it in `__enter__` and `__exit__`, e.g. `with nzf:`
    - **Why?** because multiple threads can manipulate per object state, affects other places, primarily an issue since `process_nw_worker` was added with multiple connections in different threads working on the same nzf, remove items from a list/dict, or iterate, and weird things can happen.
    - I think `TRYLIST_LOCK` is likely overused, contention on a single lock when the changes it's protecting don't conflict, but again I didn't want to fix it elsewhere in this PR.
    - n.b. I'm not sure I'm convinced by `try ... except` being faster than using `__enter__` and `__exit__` except in microbenchmarks at exceptional rates, the decorator is nice but can only use global locks and not all methods that modify data are using the TRYLIST_LOCK, so it can be changed by other threads anyway.
- NzbFile
    - **articles:** changed list to a dict, O(1) removals, test_download_10GB.nzb is a good demonstration, first file is 1498 articles, excluding the first, list removals are O(n) that’s worst case 1,496 memmove calls for 1,119,756 elements, we generally download in order so worse case is common for us.
    - **remove_article**
        - locks articles
        - removed the `article in self.articles` check and take advantage of dict pop method, single operation to remove if present, rather than searching and searching again for a removal (although cheaper now with dicts)
    - **get_articles**, **reset_all_try_lists** (not used?)
        - locks articles
    - **completed:**
        - locks articles when necessary
- General:
    - **self:** removed a few hot path uses of self by using a local variable
</details>

## Threading and Locks

Outlined in a few places above, but there are several existing not thread safe operations. I expect I'm finding them because it's trying to operate so fast.

I've had failures at `sabnzbd.ArticleCache.purge_articles` `Set changed size during iteration` `@synchronized(NZO_LOCK)` is not sufficient, an API request can remove an item while something else saves to the cache. There are many similar issues throughout the codebase, I intend to address them in a separate PR.

A common "fix" appears to be shallow copying objects instead of fixing the underlying threading issues, this is poor for performance and not safe. "Not locked for performance" doesn't pass the smell test, it's not safe if something else can modify the object while it's being used. When not handled correctly it can cause undefined state changes.

I expect there are ways test for thread safety issues, it's more likely an issue with many receive threads or the free-threaded model in the future.

## Does it work?

You tell me; testing on a local 10 Gbps network using `tc` with a 20ms delay, 16 connections, 5 pipelining, on a now 5-year-old desktop (5800x) has gone from 600 MBs to 1000 MBs, but it needs some real-world testing to see how real servers respond.

An **RPI4** shows a **100%** speed increase with the optimisations and pipelining makes little to no difference, it is bottlenecked by lack of hardware acceleration for TLS decoding.

An **i5 14500** shows minor **5-10%** increase with no pipelining, purely the CPU usage optimisations. Pipelining at even low levels 2-5 is extremely beneficial at low and high latency **100-300%** but too high is detrimental.

pullRequest.title: Prevent filepath creation before first article is processed
Fix read/write actions after reset_nw
pullRequest.body:
I think this fixes #3222

I tested it by placing self.reset_nw calls between read and writes here, simulating such call occuring in read.

 https://github.com/sabnzbd/sabnzbd/blob/c4211df8dc8ae52bcdf19f700d45620083f0d681/sabnzbd/downloader.py#L741-L746

It's a little odd the newswrapper instance gets reused instead of replaced, but I think this is ok, the socket is already unregistered so won't be selected again.

pullRequest.title: Make behaviour after reset more robust
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3226

This adds checks in several places that the connection has not been reset since the select operation was enqueued. It also checks before writing and checks if _response_queue is empty before popleft.
I called it `reset_gen` rather than `resets` or similar because I wanted to avoid indicating that it's allowed to reset it to 0.

pullRequest.title: Fix failing downloader tests and make tests less fragile
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3250

There were a few issues in the downloader logic:

- "Timed Out" in logs, was a race where idle_threads/busy_threads collided so a socket could be removed from listening but in busy_threads
- `nw.timeout = None` was missing which prevented kick starting idle threads, it used to be in soft_reset, that still kind of happens but it's remove_socket now.
- The biggest issue was if 8 connections were initially made, but they do not all immediately have article requests queued, they return to idle_threads. Then there were bugs related to those idle connections having not completed the authentication flow so not `connected` and not getting more work assigned.
- Split the logic for queuing the next request for a server and allow downloader to call it to kick start requests.

Test improvements:

- Made `download_nzb` helper less fragile
  - Clear queue/history between tests
  - When random job names are used use `"TestDownload_%s" % str(uuid.uuid4()).replace("-", "")` so deobfuscator won't get involved and they are easier to see in logs instead of many timestamps within 5 minutes of each other
  - Use API instead of selenium for reliability, it was matching name, but the name requested isn't always what it is created as
  - Sleep less between checks = quicker tests
  - Log more meaningful information about failure, queue, history, and completed files.
- Because download_nzb is less fragile I have removed flaky from download tests, it hid the failures
- Fixed assertions in `test_functional_adding_nzbs_clean.py` - it's where I copied the api tests from and found its assertions failed after 10 seconds and it carried on anyway, it does it twice so that's ~20 seconds saved

Shaves a few minutes off the test times, removing time.sleep(1) helps a lot.

`test_download_unicode_made_on_windows` fails on all platforms because it tries to deobfuscate, which used to waste 20 seconds due to flaky, so only ~10 seconds now. Will recreate it as a separate PR.

---

I ran test_functional_downloads.py 6000 times overnight without a failure.

pullRequest.title: Fix connection test failing when bad credentials are used
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3255

Sometime sabnews could handle authinfo so that we can test it.

pullRequest.title: Adjust monitored socket events as required to prevent hot looping
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3260 #3266

`prepare_request` returns True if there is a request to send buffered or there is a request already in-flight we are waiting for a response for. So if it's False, the connection can be idled.

The problem was once all articles are downloaded, the connection was still active because the force disconnection isn't until the queue is empty. So select was returning the sockets as available for writing hundreds of times per second, starving the assembler of cpu resources.

The main culprit was this logic was incorrect, it waited until timeout.

```py
not self.next_request
and not self._response_queue
and (not server.active or server.restart or not self.timeout or time.time() > self.timeout)
```

pullRequest.title: Fix queue cannot be loaded
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3269

I've used nzf level locks, it makes setting them simpler.

Part I missed initially is that `super().__setstate__(dict_.get("try_list", []))` is called from file and needs `self.lock` on article, so it needs to come after the locks have been restored.

I think I could make an argument for at least the lock initialisation lines being executed before anything else in `__setstate__` methods.

I've tried this with:
- Items added to the queue with no downloading
- Items added and partially downloaded, so trylist is restored

pullRequest.title: Fix adding sockets mid-connect
pullRequest.body: Fixes #3288 mostly reverts to 4.5.5 behaviour: https://github.com/sabnzbd/sabnzbd/blob/11ba9ae12ade8c8f2abb42d44ea35efdd361fae5/sabnzbd/downloader.py#L609-L617

pullRequest.title: Fix server retention not always applied
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3302

The peek option sees if there is a request available without consuming it from `article_queue` but peek was not applying the retention preference which will result subsequent calls taking articles from the prefetch bypassing the retention check entirely and potentially going to an ineligible server.

pullRequest.title: Make conditional_cache exception safe
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3311

This is still not thread-safe, that is two threads could both calculate and store a value in the cache.
But it's not necessary that it is, simply adjusting it so during a race it doesn't raise exceptions is good enough.

Also exclude force from the key and removes a TTL addition on each lookup

```py
# Excluding force from the key resolves this issue

diskspace()
# WRITE SOMETHING
a = diskspace(force=True)
b = diskspace()
assert a == b
```

pullRequest.title: Fix race when direct unpacker is killed
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3347

AI mostly found it after feeding it the symptoms and log.

> The race is between run()'s final cleanup and add() checking state. Here's the sequence:
>
>1. run() calls self.reset_active() (line 355) → sets active_instance = None
>2. add() acquires START_STOP_LOCK, enters
>3. check_requirements() checks self.killed → still False → passes
>4. Line 151: not self.active_instance → True (reset in step 1)
>5. Meanwhile run() finishes, sets self.killed = True (line 361), returns → thread dies
>6. Back in add(): not self.is_alive() → True (thread just died)
>7. self.start() → crash — thread already ran once
>
> The root cause: run()'s final cleanup (lines 355–361) is not protected by START_STOP_LOCK, so add() can observe a half-updated state — active_instance is None and killed is still False, and is_alive() flips to False between the two checks.

The order doesn't matter but it is required that both `self.reset_active()` and `self.killed = True` are called under the lock.

pullRequest.title: Fix checking for unwanted files in rars once password is known
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #3358

Currently on subsequent calls to `check_encrypted_and_unwanted_files` the previously checked and confirmed password is not passed to rarfile therefore in the case of RARs using header encryption the file list is not available to be checked for unwanted files.
